Title: Delila and Rosea1 induce anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit and extend lifespan of cancer-susceptible mice

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant activity; associated with extension of lifespan in cancer-susceptible mice fed anthocyanin-rich tomato powder
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: anthocyanin biosynthetic genes in the flavonoid/phenylpropanoid pathway
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates anthocyanin pathway genes when coexpressed with Rosea1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit when coexpressed with Rosea1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic fruit-specific expression under E8 promoter; metabolite profiling of anthocyanins and phenylpropanoids; gene expression analysis by suppression subtractive hybridization, northern blot, and microarray
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: fruit-specific expression during tomato fruit development/ripening
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Delila cDNA expressed under the fruit-specific E8 promoter in tomato contributed to induction of anthocyanin accumulation in fruit. 2. Delila functioned as a regulatory factor rather than a biosynthetic enzyme. 3. In combination with Rosea1, it activated anthocyanin-related transcriptional programs and increased antioxidant capacity of tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The experimentally manipulated regulatory gene is from the origin species Antirrhinum majus, whereas the metabolic phenotype was studied in tomato. The paper content provided here is mainly Methods, so detailed direct target genes and localization are not explicitly stated in the supplied text.
